question: How much gold does India have, and why can't we stop buying it?

theme.label: Economy and public money
readerQuestions: What is the headline economic number?
Is this a total number or a per-person number?
How much of the story is prices, exchange rates, or GDP share?
What does this explain about household life?

plannedCharts.title: India's private gold mountain
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The headline answer: Indians privately hold more gold than almost any nation on earth.

plannedCharts.title: What the RBI keeps in the vault
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Set the private hoard against the state's: the central bank's gold is tiny next to households'.

plannedCharts.title: The RBI has been buying real metal
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Strip out the price and the RBI is genuinely accumulating gold, not just watching its value rise.

plannedCharts.title: The RBI is quietly buying more gold
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Gold is a growing slice of how India holds its foreign reserves.

plannedCharts.title: Half a gram per Indian, every year
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The obsession, divided across 1.4 billion people.

plannedCharts.title: India's gold bill, in rupees, keeps climbing
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Gold is one of India's largest imports, and the rupee bill keeps setting records.

plannedCharts.title: But the tonnage barely moved
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The record bill is mostly price, not more metal.

plannedCharts.title: The bill and the tonnage have come apart
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The clearest picture of paying more for the same gold: the bill and the metal pulling apart.

plannedCharts.title: Gold is India's second-biggest import after oil
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Gold's pull on the economy is easier to feel against India's other big imports.

plannedCharts.title: Gold helped tip India into its 2013 crisis
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Gold imports are paid for in scarce dollars, so they widen the gap between what India earns and spends abroad.

plannedCharts.title: Where India's gold comes from
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Two refining hubs supply most of it, not the mines.

plannedCharts.title: A second source agrees
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Two independent estimates of India's gold imports line up.

plannedCharts.title: Raw gold in, jewellery out
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: India is not just a sink for gold; it is a workshop that re-exports it as jewellery.

plannedCharts.title: Where India's jewellery goes
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The re-export engine's customers.

plannedCharts.title: The jewellery imports India just clamped down on
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: A quiet surge in jewellery imports provoked a 2026 policy crackdown.

plannedCharts.title: Imported versus actually bought
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Not all imported gold stays; some leaves as jewellery or is recycled.

plannedCharts.title: Jewellery is fading; investment gold is recovering
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: How Indians hold gold is shifting, but it is a recovery from a low, not a clean secular rise.

plannedCharts.title: Money is pouring into gold ETFs
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Gold is being financialised: held as a fund unit in a demat account, not only as a bangle.

plannedCharts.title: Indians are now buying gold by the rupee, on UPI
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The most modern face of the obsession: gold bought in tiny amounts, instantly, through a payments app.

plannedCharts.title: One rupee in gold versus one rupee in the stock market
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The question every Indian saver asks: would I have done better in gold or in shares?

plannedCharts.title: Stretch it to thirty years, and shares win
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The answer flips with the horizon: over a full generation, equities clearly beat gold.

plannedCharts.title: Did gold actually beat inflation?
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: A rising price is not the same as rising purchasing power.

plannedCharts.title: The tax wedge on Indian gold
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Indians pay more than the world price for gold, and policy is why.

plannedCharts.title: The duty the government keeps moving
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Behind that premium is a single policy lever the state has yanked again and again.

plannedCharts.title: Gold loans are the fastest-growing way Indians borrow
plannedCharts.subtitle: IndiaDataHub/RBI · personal-loan subcategories · Jan 2019 to Apr 2026
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Gold is no longer only stored; it is increasingly borrowed against, a fast-rising form of household credit.

selectionRules: Start from the page question, not from the indicator name.
Choose the smallest set of visuals that explains scale, trend, structure, comparison, and caveat.
Prefer mobile-friendly charts: line charts with sparse ticks, horizontal bars, share strips, pyramids only when essential, and no dense tables.
Use one strong pull quote only when the evidence supports it with a number.
Add glossary blocks for technical terms that normal readers may not know.
caveats: Use only figures present in lockedNumbers or sourceSummaries.
If a related theme indicator is present, you may use it for context, but do not pretend it is the main answer.
If source coverage is thin, say so directly.
Do not compare states, districts, or cities unless the evidence packet contains those geographies.
forbiddenClaims: Do not infer causality from a trend unless the evidence explicitly supports it.
Do not cite a source that is not present in sourceSummaries.
